content.full_text:
RAVI Mt.
C H A
R MA
FAIR POLITICAL
PRACTICES
COMMISSION
March 18,
2996
Gary J.
Grimm
California Regional Water
Quality Control Board
San Francisco Bay Region
2101 Webster Street,
Suite
500
Oakland,
CA
94612
Re:
Your Request for Assistance
Our File No.
1-96-069
Dear Mr.
Grimm:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
concerning the “revolving door” restrictions of the Political
Reform Act (the “Act”)
1 that will apply to you after you leave
your employment with the State Water Resources Control Board.
Since your advice request does not refer to a specific
governmental decision,
we are treating your request as one for
informal assistance.
2
QUESTIONS
1.
With regard to the prohibitions in Section 87406,
which
agency would be considered to be the agency for which you “worked
or represented”?
2.
What restrictions does the Act impose upon your post—
government employment activities with respect to contacts with
other state agencies,
including other regional boards?
3.
May you serve as a private mediator to provide neutral
third-party facilitation of disputes involving private parties and
the State and/or Regional Board and would either the prohibitions
of Section 87401 or 87406 apply?
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000—18995.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
iminunity.provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section
428 1 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-96—069
Page2
CONCLUSION
1.
The revolving door provisions of the Act apply to the
“state administrative agency” for which you worked,
the State
Water Resources Control Board or any agency which the Board
controls.
2.
You will be subject to two types of restrictions:
o
For one year after you leave your employment with the
State Water Resources Control Board,
you may not represent any
other person for compensation before that agency.
This
prohibition would preclude you from making any formal or informal
appearance,
or any oral or written communication,
if the
appearance or communication is for the purpose of influencing the
department’s administrative or legislative action.
You also may
not represent a person in any action or proceeding involving the
issuance,
amendment,
awarding,
or revocation of a permit,
license,
grant,
or contract,
or the sale or purchase of goods or property
(referred to hereinafter as “any specific action or proceeding”).
However, this ban does not prohibit you from influencing
administrative or legislative action or any action or proceeding
before other state agencies, the Governor,
or the Legislature.
However,
“influencing” your former agency includes contacts made
with your former agency while appearing before other state
agencies,
the Governor,
or the Legislature, if the appearance is
made for the purpose of influencing your former agency.
o
In addition,
you would be permanently prohibited from
representing another person or aiding,
advising,
counseling,
consulting or assisting in representing another person before any
court or state administrative agency
(or officer or employee
thereof)
in any judicial or other proceeding in which you
participated as a state administrative official.
3.
Serving as a private mediator is not prohibited by the
Act.
However,
the permanent ban also prohibits your involvement
in any proceeding,
or specific matter between two parties,
in
which you participated while working in the Office of Chief
Counsel as a staff counsel at the State Water Resources Control
Board.
You are prohibited from participating in such proceedings
at the State Water Resources Control Board or any other state
agency.
FACTS
You have been staff counsel in the Office of the Chief
Counsel for the State Water Resources Board.
Your primary
responsibility is to provide legal services and to advise the
California Regional Water Quality Control Board,
San Francisco Bay
Region (Regional Board).
You advise the Regional Board staff and
members on legal matters relating to the Board’s regulatory
activities.
You do not work with or advise any other regional
boards.


Our File No.
1-96—069
Page
3
It is your intention to leave your employment with the State
Water Resources Control Board and to establish a private
consulting or law practice in the area of environmental law.
ANALYSIS
An official who has left state service is subject to two
types of restrictions under the Act as discussed below.
3
One—Year Ban
The first type of restriction is a one-year prohibition on
making any appearance before your former agency for the purpose of
influencing administrative or legislative action or any specific
action or proceeding.
Section 87406(d) (1)
of the Act provides that no officer or
designated employee of a state administrative agency:
for a period of one year after leaving
office or employment, shall,
for compensation, act
as agent or attorney for,
or otherwise represent,
any other person,
by making any formal or informal
appearance,
or by making any oral or written
communication, before any state administrative
agency,
or officer or employee thereof,
for which
he or she worked or represented during the
12
months before leaving office or employment, if the
appearance or communication is made for the purpose
of influencing administrative or legislative
action,
or influencing any action or proceeding
involving the issuance,
amendment,
awarding,
or
revocation of a permit,
license, grant,
or
contract,
or the sale or purchase of goods or
property.
For purposes of this paragraph,
an
appearance before a state administrative agency
does not include an appearance in a court of law,
before an administrative law judge,
or before the
Worker’s Compensation Appeals Board.”
The State Water Resources Control Board is a “state
administrative agency,” and as staff counsel,
you would be
considered an officer of that agency.
(Section 87400(a).)
Please note that the Act also prohibits state administrative
officials from making, participating in making,
or using their
official position to influence any governmental decision directly
relating to any person with whom the official is negotiating, or
has any arrangement concerning, prospective employment.
(Section
87407.)
Therefore, you would be precluded from making,
participating in making,
or using your official position to
influence any governmental decision relating to a prospective
employer.
(See,
Symkowick Advice Letter,
No. A-93-115.)


Our File No.
1-96—069
Paged
Pursuant to Section 87406,
for one year after you leave the
State Water Resources Control Board,
you may not,
for
compensation,
act as representative or agent for any person before
the Board
4 for the purpose of influencing administrative
5 or
legislative
6 action or any specific actions or proceedings.
(Section 87406.)
Based on the facts you provided concerning your employment
intentions,
we are providing you the following general guidance
which may be of assistance to you.
I
am also attaching an outline
which includes a summary and specific examples pertaining to the
revolving door provisions of the Act.
Communications and Appearances:
Under Section 87406,
you
would be prohibited from making any communication or appearance to
influence any specific action or proceeding of the State Water
Resources Control Board.
Any communication such as telephone
calls or meetings with the State Water Resources Control Board’s
staff that are intended to influence administrative or legislative
action are prohibited under Section 87406.
(Byrne Advice Letter,
No.
A—95—337.)
However,
communications with the Board which are not for the
purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action are
not restricted by Section 87406.
For example,
you could attend
informational meetings with the State Water Resources Control
Board,
or request information from the Board concerning existing
laws, regulations,
or policies,
so long as you do not attempt to
influence administrative or legislative action.
(See Bagatelos
Advice Letter,
No.
1—91—202;
and Regulation 18202(a) (1).)
You have not provided information concerning all the boards or
commissions which may be considered part of your “state
administrative agency.”
For purposes of our analysis, it would
include entities which your agency controls.
(Monagan Advice
Letter,
No. A-93-473.)
“Administrative action” is defined in Section 82002 as the
proposal, drafting,
development,
consideration,
amendment,
enactment or defeat by any state agency of any rule, regulation or
other action in any rate—making proceeding or any quasi—
legislative proceeding.
6
Section 82037 defines “legislative action” as the drafting,
introduction, consideration, modification,
enactment or defeat of
any bill, resolution,
amendment, report,
nomination or other
matter by the Legislature or by either house or any committee,
subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof,
or by a member or
employee of the Legislature acting in his or her official
capacity.
“Legislative action” also means the action of the
Governor in approving or vetoing any bill.


Our File No.
1-96—069
Page
5
For the Purpose of Influencing:
The one—year ban on
influencing your former agency only applies to the “state
administrative agency” for which you worked, the State Water
Resources Control Board.
Therefore, this ban does not prohibit
you from influencing administrative or legislative action or any
specific action or proceeding.
Thus,
generally,
you would not be
restricted by Section 87406
from lobbying the Legislature or
Governor regarding legislation.
(See Witherspoon Advice Letter,
No.
A—94—371;
Craven Advice Letter,
A—93—057.)
However,
“influencing legislative or administrative action”
includes influencing by any means,
including but not limited to
the provision or use of information, statistics,
or analyses.
(Section 82032.)
Therefore,
“influencing legislative or
administrative action” includes contacts. made with your former
agency while appearing before other state agencies, the Governor,
or the Legislature, if the appearance is made for the purpose of
influencing your former agency.
Employment Activities:
The Act’s revolving door restrictions
would not prohibit you from entering into consulting agreements or
acting as a mediator with private parties so long as in the
conduct of your duties you do not attempt to influence the State
Water Resources Control Board’s administrative or legislative
action as discussed above,
or any specific action or proceeding
before other state agencies, the Governor,
or the Legislature.
(Byrne Advice Letter, supra.)
Permanent Prohibition
The second restriction is
a permanent prohibition on
influencing any judicial, quasi-judicial,
or similar proceeding in
which the official participated while in state service.
This is a
ban on “switching sides.”
Sections 87401 and 87402 provide:
No former state administrative official, after
the termination of his or her employment or term of
office, shall for compensation act as agent or
attorney for,
or otherwise represent,
any other
person (other than the State of California)
before
any court or state administrative agency or any
officer or employee thereof by making any formal or
informal appearance,
or by making any oral or
written communication with the intent to influence,
in connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or
other proceeding if both of the following apply:
(a)
The State of California is a party or
has a direct and substantial interest.
(b)
The proceeding is one in which the
former state administrative official
participated.
Section 87401.


our File No.
1-96-069
Page
6
No former state administrative official, after
the termination of his or her employment or term of
office shall for compensation aid,
advise,
counsel,
consult or assist in representing any other person
(except the State of California)
in any proceeding
in which the official would be prohibited from
appearing under Section 87401.
Section 87402.
However, this restriction is limited to ludicial,
quasi—
judicial or other proceedings in which you participated.
Section
87400 provides:
(c)
“Judicial, quasi-judicial or other
proceeding” means any proceeding, application,
request for a ruling or other determination,
contract,
claim,
controversy,
investigation,
charge,
accusation, arrest or other particular
matter involving a specific party or parties in any
court or state administrative agency,
including but
not limited to any proceeding governed by Chapter
5
(commencing with Section 11500)
of Division
3 of
Title
2 of the Government Code.
(d)
“Participated” means to have taken part
personally and substantially through decision,
approval, disapproval,
formal written
recommendation,
rendering advice on a substantial
basis,
investigation or use of confidential
information as an officer or employee,
but
excluding approval,
disapproval or rendering of
legal advisory opinions to departmental or agency
staff which do not involve a specific party or
parties.
Proceedings Before Any Court or Other State Administrative
Agency:
The permanent ban of Sections 87401 and 87402 applies
only to judicial,
quasi—judicial or other proceedings before any
court or state administrative agency in which you participated
while at the State Water Resources Control Board.
Section
87400(a),
however,
expressly defines “state administrative agency”
to exclude the Legislature.
Participation:
As staff counsel from the State Water
Resources Control Board,
you are deemed to have “participated” in
any proceeding which you supervised or any proceeding which you
participated personally and substantially which was initiated or
pending, during your employment with the State Water Resources
Control Board.
(Byrne Advice Letter,
supra;
Brown Advice Letter,
No.
A—91—033.)


Our File No.
1-96—069
Page7
Proceeding Involving Specific Parties:
Under Section
87400(c)
set forth above,
a “proceeding” includes any particular
matter involving a specific party or parties in a state
administrative agency.
For example,
a lawsuit would be considered
a judicial,
quasi—judicial or other proceeding involving specific
parties.
(Witherspoon Advice Letter,
supra.)
In addition,
any
contractual arrangements between the State Water Resources Control
Board and a private party would constitute a proceeding in which
you participated at the Board.
I trust this information will assist you.
If you have any
additional questions, please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Lynda Doherty
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

license,
grant,
or contract,
or the sale or purchase of goods or property
(referred to hereinafter as “any specific action or proceeding”).
However, this ban does not prohibit you from influencing
administrative or legislative action or any action or proceeding
before other state agencies, the Governor,
or the Legislature.
However,
“influencing” your former agency includes contacts made
with your former agency while appearing before other state
agencies,
the Governor,
or the Legislature, if the appearance is
made for the purpose of influencing your former agency.
o
In addition,
you would be permanently prohibited from
representing another person or aiding,
advising,
counseling,
consulting or assisting in representing another person before any
court or state administrative agency
(or officer or employee
thereof)
in any judicial or other proceeding in which you
participated as a state administrative official.
3.
Serving as a private mediator is not prohibited by the
Act.
However,
the permanent ban also prohibits your involvement
in any proceeding,
or specific matter between two parties,
in
which you participated while working in the Office of Chief
Counsel as a staff counsel at the State Water Resources Control
Board.
You are prohibited from participating in such proceedings
at the State Water Resources Control Board or any other state
agency.
embedding.first_500_words: RAVI Mt. C H A R MA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION March 18, 2996 Gary J. Grimm California Regional Water Quality Control Board San Francisco Bay Region 2101 Webster Street, Suite 500 Oakland, CA 94612 Re: Your Request for Assistance Our File No. 1-96-069 Dear Mr. Grimm: This is in response to your letter requesting advice concerning the “revolving door” restrictions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) 1 that will apply to you after you leave your employment with the State Water Resources Control Board. Since your advice request does not refer to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. 2 QUESTIONS 1. With regard to the prohibitions in Section 87406, which agency would be considered to be the agency for which you “worked or represented”? 2. What restrictions does the Act impose upon your post— government employment activities with respect to contacts with other state agencies, including other regional boards? 3. May you serve as a private mediator to provide neutral third-party facilitation of disputes involving private parties and the State and/or Regional Board and would either the prohibitions of Section 87401 or 87406 apply? 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000—18995. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the iminunity.provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 428 1 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660 Our File No. 1-96—069 Page2 CONCLUSION 1. The revolving door provisions of the Act apply to the “state administrative agency” for which you worked, the State Water Resources Control Board or any agency which the Board controls. 2. You will be subject to two types of restrictions: o For one year after you leave your employment with the State Water Resources Control Board, you may not represent any other person for compensation before that agency. This prohibition would preclude you from making any formal or informal appearance, or any oral or written communication, if the appearance or communication is for the purpose of influencing the department’s administrative or legislative action. You also may not represent a person in any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property (referred to hereinafter as “any specific action or proceeding”). However, this ban does not prohibit you from influencing administrative or legislative action or any action or proceeding before other state agencies, the Governor, or the Legislature. However, “influencing” your former agency includes contacts made with your former agency while appearing before other state agencies, the Governor, or the Legislature, if the appearance is made for the purpose of influencing your former agency. o In addition, you would be permanently prohibited from representing another person or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting
